twitter_thread: 57% of devs report LLMs already automating 30% of their workload. I see opportunity:
LLMs can process 10k lines of code/second, but fail 90% of the time on novel problems.
Double down on high-level design, testing, and validation to stay ahead.
Pytest, Unittest, and JUnit can reduce bugs by 40%. Master these tools.
Don't spend 80% of your time on implementation. LLMs can do that in 1/10th the time.
Upskill in AI/ML engineering, data science, or cybersecurity to boost earnings by 25%.
What's the most impactful skill you've acquired to stay relevant in the AI era? #aiengineering #careergoals
pack.linkedin: Large Language Models are automating 30% of software engineering tasks, but this shift creates new opportunities. By focusing on high-level design, testing, and validation, and using tools like Pytest and Unittest, devs can reduce bugs by 40% and increase efficiency. What's the most important skill you've acquired to stay ahead in this new landscape?
tiktok_hooks: LLMs just automated 30% of my job, and I'm thrilled
The 1 skill that makes you LLM-proof as a dev
Why I'm betting on human intuition over LLMs
pack.reddit_headline: Software engineers: will LLMs replace us or create new opportunities for growth?
